1: When he was in the wilderness of Judah — the superscription locates this psalm in a specific biographical moment. Two major wilderness periods fit: David's years fleeing Saul in the Judean desert around En-gedi and Maon (1 Sam 23-26), or his flight from Jerusalem during Absalom's revolt through the wilderness east of the city (2 Samuel 15:23-16:14). The Judean wilderness — the harsh rain-shadow desert between Jerusalem and the Dead Sea — is the setting. The three-fold intensification ("my soul thirsts... my flesh longs") is the most physicalized expression of spiritual longing in the Psalter: spiritual hunger described in terms of dehydration and bodily craving.
2: So I have gazed upon you in the sanctuary, beholding your power and your glory — the sanctuary is the place where YHWH's kavod (glory/presence) was concentrated and visible. The Tabernacle (and later the Temple) was the architectural embodiment of this principle: YHWH's visible, experienceable presence was accessible in the sanctuary in a way it was not in the wilderness. The psalmist's thirst (v1) is partly resolved by memory of sanctuary encounter: even in the waterless wilderness, the remembered vision of divine glory sustains the soul.
3: Your steadfast love is better than life itself — this is among the most radical theological claims in the Psalter. In ancient Israelite thought, life (hayyim) was the highest good — everything in the covenant system (blessings, inheritance, descendants, health) was understood as fullness of life. To say that hesed (covenant loyalty) surpasses life itself is to place the covenant relationship above the life it sustains. This anticipates the NT's claim that to know God is eternal life (John 17:3) and Paul's "to live is Christ and to die is gain" (Philippians 1:21).
4: In your name I will lift up my hands — the physical posture of prayer with hands raised toward heaven was common across the ancient Near East. Egyptian, Mesopotamian, and Israelite prayer art all depict raised hands as the gesture of petition and devotion. Lifting hands toward the Name (shem) situates the prayer within the theology of divine presence mediated through YHWH's name: the name was understood as a real extension of his person, present in the sanctuary (Deuteronomy 12:11; 1 Kings 8:29). Lifting hands toward the Name is lifting hands toward the dwelling of YHWH's effective presence.
5: My soul is satisfied as with the richest of food — the imagery of the todah (thanksgiving) banquet. When a worshiper's prayer was answered, they were expected to return to the temple with a todah sacrifice; the offering included leavened bread, and the meat was eaten at a communal meal with invited guests (Psalms 22:26; Leviticus 7:11-15). "The richest of food" (chelev vadeshen, fat and abundance) describes the quality of this sacred meal. The psalmist's spiritual satisfaction in the wilderness is imagined in terms of the fellowship meal of answered prayer that awaits in the sanctuary.
6: I meditate on you through the watches of the night — the ancient Israelite night was divided into three watches (Judges 7:19: beginning of the middle watch; Lamentations 2:19: beginning of the night watches; Exodus 14:24: the morning watch). Later, Roman military practice introduced four watches, which the NT uses (Mark 13:35). The Psalter frequently locates the most intense divine encounters in the night hours (Psalms 16:7; Psalms 17:3; Psalms 42:8; Psalms 119:62). Night meditation on YHWH was both a devotional practice and a practical necessity in the heat of the Judean wilderness.
7: In the shadow of your wings I will rejoice — the wings imagery has a dual referent in Israelite theology: (1) the cherubim of the ark of the covenant, whose outstretched wings formed YHWH's throne (Exodus 25:20; 1 Chronicles 28:18; Psalms 80:1; Psalms 99:1); and (2) the mother-bird imagery of divine protection (Deuteronomy 32:11 — "like an eagle that stirs up its nest and hovers over its young"; Psalms 91:4). Both images speak to the protective presence of YHWH: under the wings of the cherubim-throne, and sheltered by the mother-bird of Deut 32. In the wilderness, away from the ark, the psalmist claims that protection as still available.
8: My soul clings close to you — the verb dabaq (to cling, cleave, hold fast) is the specific covenant-loyalty verb of Deuteronomy. Deuteronomy 10:20: "Fear the LORD your God and dabaq to him"; Deuteronomy 11:22: "to dabaq to him"; Deuteronomy 30:20: "dabaq to him — for he is your life." This is the same verb used in Genesis 2:24 for the husband's bond to his wife. The psalmist describes his relationship with YHWH in the wilderness using the covenant-loyalty language of Deuteronomy: not sentiment but the formal, embodied attachment of the covenant partner who refuses to let go.
9: Those who seek to destroy my life shall descend into the depths of the earth — "the lower parts of the earth" (tahtiyyot ha-aretz) is Sheol, the underworld realm of the dead (Ezekiel 26:20; Psalms 31:14-18; Psalms 32:18-32 use the same phrase for descent to the underworld). In ancient Israelite cosmology, the earth had three levels: the heavens above, the earth's surface, and Sheol beneath. Those who threaten the psalmist's life will themselves go where life ceases — the reversal of fate is a consistent pattern in the lament psalms (Psalms 7:15-16; Psalms 35:8; Psalms 57:6).
10: They will be delivered over to the sword; they will become prey for jackals — the shame of unburied death. In the ancient world, proper burial was a fundamental requirement of social dignity; to be left unburied and consumed by scavengers was the ultimate humiliation (1 Samuel 17:44-46 — Goliath's threat; Jeremiah 7:33; Psalms 8:2; Psalms 16:4). Jackals (shualim) were the familiar scavengers of the Palestinian landscape, associated with desolation and the aftermath of judgment. The enemies who threatened the psalmist's life will suffer the most disgraceful possible end: no burial, no memorial, consumed by animals.
11: The king will rejoice in God / the mouths of liars will be silenced — the royal dimension surfaces at the psalm's close. The "king" is either David himself (if the psalm is autobiographical) or the Davidic king as the representative of the covenant community. His vindication by God produces joy (yismaḥ). "All who swear by him will boast in him" places the king's vindication within the community's covenant oath-structure: those who have sworn their loyalty to YHWH will share in the vindication. The silencing of liars completes the reversal: the double-speaking enemies of v4 find their mouths closed.
